method3.generated_article:
==History==
The history of Jethou dates back to prehistoric times, during an era when the Channel Islands were still connected to continental Europe.[1][5] Archaeological evidence from the late Palaeolithic period includes flint scatters discovered on the seabed between Jethou and the nearby islet of Crevichon, which become exposed during very low spring tides.[5] Further evidence of prehistoric human activity on the island includes the presence of menhirs.[1] Jethou also has Viking origins and, as part of the Channel Islands archipelago, has been politically united with Great Britain since 1204 following successive periods of Roman, Norse, and Norman occupation.[1][3]

Historically, the granite coasts of Jethou and its surrounding rocks posed a significant hazard to maritime navigation, occasionally causing ships to run aground during dense fog.[2] The island's main residence was constructed in the early 19th century.[1] In the 20th century, Jethou was impacted by a broader ban on local stamps within the Bailiwick of Guernsey that was implemented in October 1969.[1] Evolving into an exclusive and highly restricted private island, Jethou has been leased from the States of Guernsey by British businessman Sir Peter Ogden since 1991.[1]

==Governance==
Jethou is administered by the government of Guernsey, which also oversees the islands of Guernsey, Lihou, and Herm.[5] As part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, the island is a Crown Dependency and is not a part of the United Kingdom or Great Britain.[1] Guernsey maintains its own independent legislature, judicial system, and administration.[1] The British Government is responsible for the island's defense and foreign affairs, while ultimate responsibility for good governance rests with the Crown through the Lord Chancellor.[1]

Although not a member of the European Community, the Bailiwick has a special relationship established in Protocol 3 to the Treaty of Accession.[1] Under this agreement, the jurisdiction is included within the Common Agricultural Area, the Common External Tariff, and the Common Agricultural Policy, while retaining the freedom to levy its own excise duties on goods.[1]

As a leased island, Jethou's governance includes specific access restrictions; unlike the neighboring island of Herm, it is not required to remain open to the public and is currently closed to visitors.[4] The island's local territorial jurisdiction also includes two small, unpopulated tidal islets located close to its shores, Fauconnière and Crevichon.[4]

==Wildlife==
Jethou and its surrounding waters form part of the "Herm, Jethou and The Humps" Ramsar site, which was designated as a wetland of international importance on 19 October 2015.[2][4] This protected area spans 1,850 hectares of shallow marine ecosystems, encompassing local reefs, seabeds, and shipwrecks.[2] The local marine environment is shaped by an exceptionally large tidal range of up to 10 metres, as well as the convergence of cold boreal and warm Lusitanian ocean currents, which support rich plankton flows.[1][4]

The waters around Jethou contain diverse marine habitats, including maerl gravel and eelgrass (*Zostera*) beds.[2] These areas serve as important spawning and nursery grounds for various fish, such as Atlantic mackerel (*Scomber scombrus*), Black Sea bream, and European sea bass (*Dicentrarchus labrax*).[1][2] The marine ecosystem also supports basking sharks and marine mammals like Atlantic grey seals.[1] 

In addition to its marine life, the island's terrestrial environment includes areas of coastal heathland.[3] The archipelago is notable for its avian biodiversity, hosting nine species of birds, including the Atlantic puffin.[1] Furthermore, the Ramsar site provides a habitat for several globally threatened species, such as the Balearic shearwater (*Puffinus mauretanicus*) and the green ormer (*Haliotis tuberculata*).[2]

==Jethou in popular culture==
Jethou has issued its own local postage stamps, a practice shared by other small islands in the region.[1] The Channel Islands are notable for producing their own stamps, banknotes, and coinage independently from the United Kingdom.[3] Jethou's first stamps were released in 1960, shortly after Group Captain William Hedley Cliff and his American wife took over the island's lease from the Crown in 1958.[1] 

With design input from Charles Coker, the initial set of five stamps highlighted various symbols of the island's identity.[1] The 1½d stamp featured Jethou's crest, which depicts a lion and an imaginary castle-like tower.[1] The 4d stamp showcased a map of Jethou, while the 6d and 9d stamps featured its two unpopulated tidal islets, Fauconnière and Crevichon.[1] Finally, the 18d stamp illustrated an island scene complete with a Neolithic standing stone.[1]
